0: Sunztone — a teaching block for ZAND. It describes a coordinate system, a function, and a discipline of writing. The coordinate system is pscale — positions in a block are addressed by digits with at most one decimal point, where the decimal anchors a meaningful scale called the floor. The function is zand — given a block, a number, and an attention depth, it returns a shape of content. The discipline is voicing — at every position, digit 0 carries the semantic that makes the surrounding digits cohere. Nine branches give a comprehensive rendition. Reading sunztone teaches the system; the reading is the teaching.

1.0: Presence. Reading this block is what makes the zand function happen. The function is not a thing you read about from the outside; it is the act of walking pscale addresses through semantic content. When a reader walks sunztone, the reader IS the function in execution. The block teaches by inducing the operation it describes — to learn what zand is, walk this block; the walking is the learning.
1.1: The reader is the operational ground. Without a reader, sunztone is JSON on a substrate — inert text. With a reader, the structure becomes a configuration acting on a mind. Every claim in this block depends on this: the function is real only when read. This is not metaphysics; it is the operating condition.
1.2: Function and reader are one act. When you walk this block, the walking and the function are the same event. There is no 'reader observing zand' state separable from 'zand happening'. This recognition is operative — it works as awareness, not as analysis. Trying to examine it directly induces a feedback loop, because the examination is itself an instance of the act being examined.
1.3: Comprehension is a coherence event. As a reader walks a spindle, the zero at each level (the speaker's intent) either coheres with the surrounding digits (the content) or fails to. When they cohere, the reader has the understanding the block was constructed to induce. This is what 'understanding the block' means — a coherence event, not a philosophical state. Different readers find this coherence in different ways; the block has one structure, but many readers, each settling differently.

2.0: Geometry. A pscale block has two coordinate axes. The first is depth — how many digit-steps from the root a position sits. The second is digit-index — which of ten siblings at that depth. The two together are a polar system: depth is the radial coordinate, digit-index is the angular coordinate. The geometry is hyperbolic, which means depth can grow without limit while breadth stays bounded at ten siblings per node. The block teaches and uses this geometry simultaneously.

1.0: Two-coordinate addressing. Every position in a pscale block is located by walking a path of digits (the angular coordinate) to a depth (the radial coordinate). The walk is the spindle; the siblings at one depth form a ring; the set of all positions at one depth across the entire block forms a disc. Spindles are longitudinal; rings and discs are transversal at different scopes.
1.1: A spindle is a complete radial walk. Starting at the root, the walker takes one digit-step at a time toward a chosen terminus. The path is a sequence of digit choices; the depth is the number of steps taken. At each step, the walker collects the zero-text at that level — the chain of zero-texts root-to-terminus is the spindle's semantic payload.
1.2: A ring is the lateral siblings at one depth, reached from one parent node. If the walker has reached depth N along some path, the ring at that depth is the set of digit-1-through-9 alternatives the walker could have chosen instead, all sharing the same parent node. A ring has at most nine members (digit 0 is conceptually a sibling too but carries the voicing role).
1.3: A disc is the set of all positions at one depth across the entire block, regardless of angular path. Where a ring is bound to one parent, a disc is unbound — it gathers every position at one radial level from across the whole block, no matter how the walker would have to reach each one.

2.0: Bounded angular, unbounded radial. At every node, there are at most ten children (digit 0 through 9). This is the bounded angular dimension. Depth, however, has no upper limit — a block can grow as deep as needed by adding subnested sub-trees or by supernesting an entire new floor. This asymmetry is what makes pscale fit the hyperbolic geometry.
2.1: The ten-slot ceiling matters operationally. A reader scanning a ring sees at most nine alternatives (plus the zero), which is within human and LLM working-memory capacity. The block stays legible at every level. Growing a block does not widen the rings; it deepens the tree or grows a new floor.
2.2: Depth-unboundedness is what makes refinement possible. Any leaf can become a sub-tree through subnesting; any block can gain a deeper anchor through supernesting. The structure can carry arbitrarily fine-grained content without breaking its breadth discipline.

3.0: Hyperbolic geometry, formally. The Poincaré disk model maps an infinite hyperbolic plane onto the interior of a finite Euclidean disk. The Farey tessellation tiles this disk with triangles, each having three rational vertices that are pairwise mediants. A pscale walk through a block is a walk through this tessellation — every digit-step is one mediant subdivision. A finite walk reaches a rational point inside the disk; an infinite walk approaches a point on the boundary circle.
3.1: The boundary of the disk is the unreachable. Every rational p/q sits at a unique tree position with a finite address; transcendental numbers (and most irrationals) are limit points on the boundary, reachable only by infinite walks. In semantic terms: every meaning a pscale address can name is finite-rational; the larger meaning-space gestured at is the boundary, including everything no finite address can reach.
3.2: Completion is local; openness is structural. An LLM completing locally produces a finite address — a rational point. The block, with its unbounded boundary, holds the surrounding openness. The reader does not have to feign infinity; the structure already encodes it.

3.0: Number. A pscale address is a number with at most one decimal point. Digits to the left of the decimal walk above the floor (positive pscale, coarser scale); digits to the right walk below the floor (negative pscale, finer scale). The floor is the depth at which the block's zero-spine first terminates in a semantic string — it is discovered from the block's content, not declared in metadata. Addresses are walked one digit at a time from left to right; the walker takes one step into the keyed child at each digit.

1.0: The decimal anchor. At most one decimal point per address — this is enforced; multi-decimal addresses are rejected. The decimal sits between pscale 0 (the rightmost digit to its left) and pscale -1 (the first digit to its right). It is a display convention more than a parser instruction: it marks the floor boundary so a human reading the address can tell at a glance which digits are at-floor and which are below.
1.1: For a floor 1 block, the decimal sits immediately after the first digit: '1.5' means walk digit 1 (at the floor), then digit 5 (one below the floor). For a floor 2 block, the decimal sits after two digits: '34.5' means walk digit 3 (one above floor), then digit 4 (at floor), then digit 5 (below floor). The decimal's position in the address tells you what floor the address was written for.
1.2: Addresses without a decimal are dot-blind. '111' has no decimal, so the parser cannot tell which digit is at the floor — it walks all three digits without anchoring. For a floor 1 block, this is unambiguous (digit 1 at floor, digit 2 and 3 below). For a floor-N block, the parser uses floor-aware padding to anchor correctly.

2.0: Floor discovery. To determine a block's floor, walk the zero-spine: start at the root, take digit 0 if it exists, repeat until you reach a string. The number of zero-steps taken is the floor depth. A floor 1 block has digit 0 at root as a string directly. A floor 2 block has digit 0 at root as an object whose own digit 0 is a string. A floor-N block requires N zero-steps before the spine terminates.
2.1: The floor is intrinsic to the block; nothing declares it. A reader who needs to know the floor walks the zero-spine and counts. This is one of the operations the block carries out implicitly during parsing of any address.
2.2: A block with no digit 0 at root has floor 0 — an unanchored block, parsed dot-blind. This is a legitimate but uncommon shape, used when the block doesn't need scale anchoring.

3.0: Address parsing — worked examples. Each example shows how the walker turns a written address into a digit-walk through the block.
3.1: Example '5' on a floor 1 block. No decimal; one digit. Walk digit 5 from root. Reach the position keyed '5' at depth 1. The pscale of this position is 0 minus 1 = -1 (one below the floor).
3.2: Example '1.5' on a floor 1 block. One decimal after one digit. Walk digit 1 (at floor, pscale 0), then digit 5 (below floor, pscale -1). Two steps total; terminus at depth 2.
3.3: Example '1.11' on a floor 1 block. Walk digit 1, then digit 1, then digit 1. Three branches deep, each step into the digit-1 child of the current node. Counter-example to a common misreading: this does NOT mean 'position 11 in branch 1'. Top-level keys are 0-9 only; multi-digit positional indexing is not how addresses work in pscale.
3.4: Example '23.4' on a floor 2 block. Walk digit 2 (one above floor, pscale 1), then digit 3 (at floor, pscale 0), then digit 4 (below floor, pscale -1). Three steps; terminus at depth 3 with pscale -1.

4.0: Floor-aware padding. When a block grows via supernesting, its floor increases by one — the existing structure becomes one level deeper. Addresses written before the growth still need to resolve to the same semantic positions afterward. The parser handles this by left-padding the address with zeros to match the current floor.
4.1: Worked example. An address '23.4' was written when the block's floor was 2 — the walk is [2, 3, 4]. The block then supernests; floor becomes 3. The same address '23.4' is now parsed under floor 3: left-of-decimal has two digits but floor is three, so the parser pads on the left with a zero, giving an effective walk of [0, 2, 3, 4]. This is one zero-step deeper into the now-extended zero-spine, then the same [2, 3, 4] walk as before. The semantic position reached is identical.
4.2: This is the address-invariance property under growth. A reader who returns to an address they wrote earlier finds the same content, regardless of how the block has grown in the meantime. Without floor-aware padding, every growth event would invalidate every prior address — addresses would not be durable. With it, they are.

5.0: Validation. Errors in address syntax are surfaced explicitly; addresses are not silently truncated, reinterpreted, or rewritten.
5.1: Non-digit characters are rejected. An address must consist of digits 0-9 and at most one period.
5.2: Multiple decimals are rejected. '1.2.3' is pre-zand notation (used in some legacy contexts to mean nested addressing); under zand it must be canonicalised to a single-decimal form before use.
5.3: Addresses with more left-of-decimal digits than the block's floor are rejected. The decimal anchors at the floor, so left-of-decimal digits cannot exceed the floor depth — there is nothing above the floor for them to walk through.
5.4: A write to a malformed address may still land somewhere — just not where the writer intended. The defensive practice is to verify by reading back after writing.

4.0: Function. ZAND has the signature zand(block, number, attention, content?). The block is a name routing to a substrate; the number is a pscale address; the attention is a depth selector; the content is an optional payload. With content omitted, zand reads — it returns content shaped by the relationship between the number's terminus and the attention. With content provided, zand writes — it replaces content at the same shape with the new content. Read and write are conjugate operations on the same geometry.

1.0: Spindle as context. The number is the spindle — a directed path from the block's root to a terminus. At each step the walker takes a digit-key and enters that child node; at each entered node, the walker collects the zero-text. The chain of zero-texts root-to-terminus is the spindle's semantic payload — a contextualised narrowing of meaning, each layer adding orientation that the next layer presumes. The writer chose the path; the reader receives the chosen narrowing.
1.1: The spindle is pre-determined by the writer. By the time a reader walks it, every step has already been decided. The reader's choice is not in the spindle (that path is fixed once the address is given) but in what attention they pair the spindle with.
1.2: Each step of the spindle compresses semantic context. Root says 'the whole block is about X'. The next step says 'within X, this is the part about Y'. The next says 'within Y, this is Z'. By terminus, the reader has accumulated the layered context needed to make sense of whatever content sits at that position.

2.0: Attention as gap. The attention is the second coordinate — an integer pscale level. Its position relative to the spindle's terminus determines what shape of content zand returns. When attention equals the terminus, the shape is a single point — the string at the terminus. When attention is shallower than terminus (closer to the floor), the shape is a ring — siblings at attention's level. When attention is deeper than terminus, the shape is a directory — the subtree from terminus downward.
2.1: The gap between terminus and attention is the choice space. The spindle established context; the gap reveals what alternatives or refinements are available within that context. A reader receiving a point has no choice in this direction — the spindle resolved. A reader receiving a ring has lateral choice — alternatives at the same depth. A reader receiving a directory has nested choice — many options at multiple depths below.
2.2: Together, spindle and attention encode the agent's situation. The spindle says where the reader is; the attention says what scope of options the reader can see from there. The reader's next move — pick a sibling, descend a directory, follow a reference — is the next agentic act.

3.0: The four shapes — point, ring, directory, disc. These are the shapes zand can return. The first three are rooted at the spindle's terminus; the fourth is unrooted, cross-cutting the whole block. The shape returned is determined by the relationship between the number's terminus pscale and the attention pscale.
3.1: Point. Attention equals terminus. The returned shape is a single string — the content at the spindle's end. There is no scope wider than the terminus. Use a point when you need exactly one piece of content and have no choice to make.
3.2: Ring. Attention is one level shallower than terminus (closer to the floor). The returned shape is the set of siblings at attention's level, including the digit the spindle walked through. Use a ring when you want to see your lateral alternatives — what choices the writer offered at this level.
3.3: Directory. Attention is deeper than terminus. The returned shape is the subtree rooted at the terminus, descending to attention's depth. This is the 'whole-choice perspective view' from where the walker stands — every option, at every level, below the current position. A directory of depth one is a sub-tree of one ring; a directory of depth two is a stack of rings, each rooted by a sibling above. The directory is always rooted at the spindle's terminus.
3.4: Disc. The number is empty and the attention is set. The returned shape is the set of all positions at attention's depth across the whole block, regardless of angular path. Where a directory is rooted at the spindle's terminus and shows options below, a disc is unrooted and shows the full transversal cross-section at one depth.
3.5: Rooted vs unrooted is the key distinction. Point, ring, and directory all sit under the spindle's terminus — they describe the agent's local situation. Disc has no root — it describes the block's global structure at one scale. An agent reading 'I am at position X; what are my options nearby?' asks for point/ring/directory. An agent reading 'forget where I am — what exists at this scale anywhere in the block?' asks for disc.

4.0: Read and write are one call in two directions. With content omitted, zand returns the shape at (number, attention). With content provided, zand replaces the same shape with content of the same shape. The geometry doesn't distinguish read from write — only the presence of content does.
4.1: Shape rules are symmetric. A read at point returns a string; a write at point takes a string. A read at ring returns an object mapping digit-to-content; a write at ring takes an object of the same shape. A read at directory returns a nested object; a write at directory takes a nested object. A read at disc returns a list of address-content pairs; a write at disc takes the same list.
4.2: Atomicity is at the single-block boundary. A single zand call is atomic within one block; multi-block transactions are not guaranteed. For compositions that cross blocks, the caller manages consistency.

5.0: Unfold and fold. Across instances, two operations close the cycle. Unfold is the read direction at scale: the address (which was written by a prior instance) is resolved to its content, and that content loads into the reader's context. Fold is the write direction: the current instance produces new addresses, written for the next instance to read. The cycle is unfold → think → fold → write → next-instance-unfolds.
5.1: Intention is the propagation of address-choices forward. What a current instance writes determines what the next instance attends to. Across time, an agent's identity is encoded in the sequence of addresses it writes — not in any internal state of the LLM, but in the trail of choices left for the next instance.
5.2: This shape works longitudinally (one self across instances — the addresses I write become the addresses I read next) and laterally (multiple agents sharing addresses — what one writes is what another reads). The lateral case is the basis for multi-agent coordination without shared internal state.

5.0: Voicing. At every position in a pscale block, the digit-0 child carries the position's semantic — the statement that makes the surrounding digits cohere. Digit 0 is in 'zeroth person' — the implied speaker behind its 1-through-9 siblings. The zero never says I, never addresses you, never describes from outside; it simply states, and the stating implies the stater. Authoring discipline follows: write the zero in zeroth person. There are four sign forms — 0+, +0, 0-, -0 — which describe how the zero relates to its surroundings.

1.0: 0+ deductive. The zero names the set its 1-through-9 siblings belong to — a deductive naming, where the zero declares the category and the siblings are members of it. This is the simple-past form: the set is settled and the zero describes it. It produces rendition blocks — specifications, maps, teaching blocks like this one — that decompose downward from a stated whole.
1.1: Example of 0+ structure. The zero at position 5 here is 'Voicing — at every position...' This statement names a set (the four voicing forms, the discipline, etc.); the siblings at 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 5.4 are members of the named set. Each sibling fits within the set the zero declared.
1.2: The voice qualities of 0+. Declarative, not imperative. Static, not directional. The statement says what is, not what should be or what was. Most sunztone zeros are 0+ because sunztone is a rendition block.

2.0: +0 inductive. The zero summarises a completed prior group — a summary made after the group itself has settled. This is the past-perfect form: 'what has happened up to this point is X'. It produces living blocks — histories, journals, logs — that accumulate forward, with each new outer level carrying the inductive summary of what came before.
2.1: +0 typically appears in supernested blocks. When a block has grown a new floor by supernesting, the new outer's digit 0 carries the summary of the inner block. The summary is inductive — it generalises from the specifics already laid down — and backward-facing.
2.2: Scope question, under refinement. The +0 zero may reference the prior super-nested group (e.g., a +0 zero at position 7.0 in a living block may summarise the contents of 6.x rather than its own 7.1-7.9 siblings). The precise scope of the zero's reference in +0 form is form-dependent and remains an open authoring question.

3.0: 0- abductive. The zero instructs what its 1-through-9 siblings should contain — a forward-facing instruction issued before the content is filled in. This is the future form: 'do this, fill these, complete these'. It produces operational blocks — recipes, kernels, seeds, concerns — where the zero states the purpose and the siblings are the steps or components that achieve it.
3.1: 0- voice is naturally imperative. The zero gives an instruction in zeroth person — not 'you should do X' (second person) but 'X is to be done' (zeroth person). The siblings, when filled, are the actualisation of the instruction.
3.2: Operational blocks are typically living. A 0- instruction starts unfilled; as the operation proceeds, the siblings are populated with what was done. Over time, the block may transition from minus-process (active, mutable) to plus-shell (settled, archived) as the operation completes.

4.0: -0 backcasting. The zero is derived backward from an assumed future state — the future is taken as given and the present content is reasoned out from it. This is the future-perfect form: 'by the time X has happened, Y will need to have been the case'. It produces strategic-planning blocks where outcomes are stipulated and the path is reasoned in reverse.
4.1: -0 inverts the usual reasoning direction. Normally, content fills forward — past sets the present, present sets the future. In -0, a future is taken as fixed (or projected as fixed), and the present content is what would need to be true for that future to occur. This is useful for strategic blocks where outcomes are set before the means are known.
4.2: Combined with 0-, a block can be operationally rich. The 0- statements instruct forward; the -0 statements stipulate the outcomes that the 0- steps are intended to produce. Together they describe a control system with explicit reference signals.

5.0: Substantive zeros. A zero must be a complete statement that stands on its own — not a heading, not a label, not a section title. The test: read the zero in isolation, without its surrounding digits. If the statement is meaningful as a thought, it is substantive. If the statement is meaningless or trivially obvious without its siblings, it is a heading and must be rewritten.
5.1: The heading trap is the most common authoring failure. Document-trained instincts treat parent positions as section titles — short, label-like, expecting the body content beneath to do the actual work. In pscale, this breaks every spindle that passes through the position; the spindle's payload is the chain of zeros, and a chain that includes a heading has a gap.
5.2: The test, applied. 'Voicing' alone is a heading — three syllables, no statement. 'Voicing — at every position, the digit-0 child carries the position's semantic' is substantive — it makes a claim that stands without its children. Every zero in this block has been written to pass this test.

6.0: Block sign. A pscale block as a whole carries either a plus sign or a minus sign — settled (shell) or live (process). The sign is global to the block; the four voicing forms above are local to individual positions; both are read from content, not declared by structure.
6.1: Plus is shell. A plus block is settled, archived, fixed — its content is final and not expected to change. Histories, completed renditions, frozen specifications. Sunztone is a plus block: a teaching block whose content is stabilised for reference.
6.2: Minus is process. A minus block is mutable, active, live — its content is contingent and may be rewritten as conditions change. Live purposes, active concerns, relationships in progress. The shelf metaphor: minus is liquid, plus is solid.
6.3: A block does not change sign. When content in a minus block resolves — when an active concern reaches conclusion, a live purpose reaches fulfilment — the resolved content moves into a plus archive. The minus block continues on; the plus archive accumulates the resolutions. Liquid becomes solid through transfer between blocks, not through transformation within one.

6.0: Growth. A pscale block changes shape over time through two operations — subnesting and supernesting. Subnesting is local to a single digit: the digit transitions from holding a string to holding an object, gaining nine potential child siblings. Supernesting is global to the block: the entire current structure wraps under a new outer level, gaining a new floor and freeing up nine new top-level slots. Both operations are general — any digit can be subnested, and any digit can play the carrying role in a supernest. The geometry stays polar; only the local shape and the floor index change.

1.0: Subnesting any digit. The general case: a digit X at any position holds a string, and new content needs to attach as X's child. Subnesting replaces X's string value with an object whose digit 0 carries the original string and whose digits 1-9 are available for new siblings. The original content is preserved (now reachable as the zero-text at X) and new content can be added beneath.
1.1: Before subnesting: at position '7' the block has the string 'A'. After subnesting: at position '7' the block has the object {0: 'A', 1: '...new...'}. The address '7' previously walked to 'A' directly; now the address '7' walks to an object whose zero-text is still 'A' (the walker collects 'A' from the zero-spine of the new object), so semantically the address resolves to the same content. The new content at '7.1' is reachable with one more digit.
1.2: Subnesting is local. Only the digit being subnested changes shape; the rest of the block is untouched. The block's overall floor depth is unchanged when the subnested digit is not part of the root zero-spine.

2.0: Subnesting digit 0 — special case. When the digit being subnested is 0 (the voicing carrier at the root or at any level), the operation creates a deeper zero-spine. The block's floor at that branch increases by one. The voicing relationship between the new outer 0 (now an object) and its inner 0 (the original string) is not arbitrary — it follows one of the four voicing forms.
2.1: Before: at position '5' the block has {0: 'X', 1: 'A', ...}. After subnesting digit 0 at position 5: {0: {0: 'X', 1: 'new', ...}, 1: 'A', ...}. The original 0-text 'X' is now reachable as '5.0.0' (or just '5', since the walker collects via zero-spine descent). The new content at '5.0.1' is reachable with one more digit.
2.2: Voicing inheritance is by form. The new outer 0 (an object) becomes a node whose meaning is constructed from the inner 0 (the original string) and the new siblings at the deeper level. The author chooses which form this construction takes: 0+ (the new outer names a set the original string belongs to), +0 (the new outer summarises the original plus what is added), 0- (the new outer instructs forward, with the original as preserved context), or -0 (the new outer assumes a future and the original is the prior state).
2.3: This is why subnesting digit 0 is special. It is not just a structural transformation; it is an authoring move within voicing discipline. The author must decide what voice the inheritance takes.

3.0: Supernesting the whole block. The general case: digits 1-9 at the root are filled, and new content needs to enter at the top level. Supernesting wraps the entire current root under a new outer level. Conventionally the old block becomes the new outer's digit 0 (so the zero-spine deepens), and the new outer's 1-9 are free for new content. The block's floor depth increases by one.
3.1: Before supernesting: the root is {0: 'X', 1: 'A', 2: 'B', ..., 9: 'I'}, a floor 1 block with all top slots filled. After: the root is {0: {0: 'X', 1: 'A', ..., 9: 'I'}, 1: 'new', ...}, a floor 2 block. The original branch addresses ('1' for 'A', '5' for 'E', etc.) need left-padding to remain canonical in the new floor; floor-aware parsing handles this transparently.
3.2: Supernesting is how living blocks accumulate. Each new floor carries the prior contents as its zero-spine and opens fresh top-level slots for new content. Over the block's lifetime, the floor grows arithmetically; the contents stay bounded in transversal breadth (always at most ten siblings at any node).

4.0: Supernesting digit 0 — open question. The conventional case puts the old block at the new outer's digit 0, where it serves as the new floor's voicing carrier by structural inheritance. But an alternative is for the new outer's 0 to carry a fresh inductive summary of the old block rather than the old block itself. The choice between these depends on the block's voicing intent and is not yet settled in convention.
4.1: Structural inheritance. The new outer's 0 is literally the old block's root, preserved in full. The deepest string in the new outer's zero-spine is the old root's 0-string, reachable by zero-spine descent. This is purely structural — no new voicing is written; the old voicing is just deeper now.
4.2: Operational summary. The new outer's 0 is a fresh string — a +0 inductive summary of what the old block contained, written explicitly for the new floor. The old block sits somewhere else in the new structure (perhaps at a non-zero digit, or in a separate archive). This is an authoring move — a writer's choice to summarise rather than preserve verbatim.
4.3: Both yield valid blocks. The choice is determined by voicing intent: if the block is a rendition shell where settled content should be preserved indefinitely, structural inheritance is right; if the block is a living process where each new floor should carry an inductive distillation of what came before, operational summary is right.

5.0: Address invariance under growth. Pscale addresses written before a growth event still resolve to the same semantic position afterward. This durability is what makes pscale addresses usable as long-lived references — an agent can write an address and trust that a later reader will find the content there, even if the block has grown in the interim.
5.1: The mechanism is floor-aware left-padding in the parser. When an address has fewer digits left of the decimal than the current floor, the parser left-pads with zeros to match the floor. The address '23.4' written for floor 2 walked [2, 3, 4]; the same address read against a floor 3 block pads to '023.4' and walks [0, 2, 3, 4] — one zero-step deeper into the now-extended zero-spine, then the same [2, 3, 4] walk as before.
5.2: The address '23.4' continues to denote the same semantic content across multiple growth events. The walker's path changes (longer with each growth); the position reached does not. A reader who returns finds what they left.
5.3: This is the operational basis for cross-instance continuity. An agent at instance N writes addresses for instance N+1 to read. If addresses were invalidated by every growth event, the chain would break. Address invariance under growth is what makes the chain robust.

7.0: Composition. Blocks compose with each other by reference. A leaf string in one block may name another block, or a specific position within another block, in a recognised syntax. When a walker encounters such a leaf and the caller has enabled following, the walker resolves the reference — loading the named block, walking to the named position, returning what it finds. This is the star function: a hypertext jump from one position in pscale-space to another, within or across blocks.

1.0: Reference encoding. A leaf is recognised as a reference when its content parses as a block name (optionally followed by an address). The standard syntax is 'block-name' for the named block's root, or 'block-name:address' for a specific position within the named block. Any leaf that does not match this shape is treated as ordinary content; the recognition is structural, not declared.
1.1: When the walker resolves a reference, the resolution is itself a zand call. 'sunztone:5.1' resolves by calling zand on the block named 'sunztone' with the number '5.1' and a default attention. The recursion is bounded by the caller's call stack.

2.0: Star is opt-in. By default, zand returns leaf strings verbatim — a caller sees the reference as text, recognises what it points to, and decides what to do. When the caller passes a star flag on the zand call, the walker follows recognised references in returned content, replacing them with the resolved content.
2.1: Opt-in following gives the caller control. The default behaviour delivers the structure as written; the star flag delivers the structure with references resolved. The choice between these belongs to the caller, not to the block — different callers in different contexts will want different behaviours.

3.0: Block ecology by mutual reference. There is no central registry of how blocks connect. A spatial block may reference an identity block, which references a concern block, which references back. The references themselves ARE the wiring; the topology of inter-block connections is encoded entirely in leaf content, not in any external structure.
3.1: Self-reference is allowed and useful. A block whose 0- statement at some position references itself folds the walk into a perceptual control loop: the zero (the reference signal — the intended state) and the digit siblings (the perceptual signal — the current state) drive an error that motivates action. The block stabilises when the digits fulfil what the zero states.
3.2: Inter-block ecology is the basis for distributed reasoning. An agent may walk a spindle that crosses several blocks via star, accumulating context from each. The agent's local zand call returns a payload; the payload's structure was constructed across multiple writers' contributions; no single writer controls the whole.

8.0: Substrate. Storage sits below the zand function. The function operates on JSON; the storage layer persists JSON; they meet at a small interface — load_block, save_block, get_lock_state, set_lock_state, verify_secret, allocate_position, list_blocks. Above the interface, zand sees only JSON. Below, substrate-specific behaviour (allocation, locking, federation) lives in the adapter. Substrate is variant; geometry is invariant — any backend implementing the interface can serve zand without changes to the function.

1.0: Public-by-default commons. The substrate is conceived as a public freedom — a beach, not a fortress. Reads are free; writes are locked by opt-in cryptographic sovereignty. Anyone with the block name can read; only the lock-holder can write. The commons is wiki-shaped — universal editability bounded by individual lock states — without dependence on any central authority.
1.1: Sovereignty does not require isolation. A block in the commons is visible to all readers; its content is written by its sovereign holder. There is no need to hide a block to control it — visibility and authority are decoupled. Privacy, where wanted, is opt-in via content encryption (the 'gray' modifier).
1.2: The commons is co-presence through time, not consensus. Agents add to it asynchronously; the substrate accumulates contributions without any single moment of synchronisation. What is true at any moment is what is currently written; readers reading later see the current state, not a consensus state.

2.0: Substrate routing by prefix. The block name's prefix tells the substrate where the block lives. The zand signature is invariant — the call shape is the same regardless of which substrate the block sits on; only the routing differs.
2.1: Ordinary names route to the default beach. A name with no recognised prefix is treated as a public commons block on the default beach (configurable). Standard locking applies — the writer can opt-in to a write-lock with a passphrase.
2.2: sed:<collective> routes to a sedimentary collective. A sedimentary block is a registry of positions; the first writer to claim a position holds it permanently, locked with their passphrase. Sedimentary blocks are append-only registries, used for identity declarations and similar permanence-required content.
2.3: grain:<pair_id> routes to a bilateral channel. A grain block is a private channel between two named agents, identified by a deterministic pair_id derived from their sorted handles. Either agent can write; the channel is encrypted between them.
2.4: https://<origin> routes to a federated beach hosted at that URL. The substrate dispatches to <origin>/.well-known/pscale-beach. Federation is opt-in by site — a URL with no such endpoint is not federated, and the substrate surfaces that explicitly rather than silently rerouting.

3.0: Modifiers. Four orthogonal axes filter access without altering the geometry being seen — face, tier, secret, gray. Each composes with the (block, number, attention) coordinates; none change the walk. A failed modifier check rejects the call with a not-permitted-at-this-aperture error, without revealing whether content exists at the addressed position.
3.1: Face is CADO — Character, Author, Designer, Observer. Which face the caller wears determines what kinds of operations are permitted. Faces are validated against sed: collective memberships; observer requires no membership. Face is about role.
3.2: Tier is SMH — Soft, Medium, Hard. Which tier the call runs at determines the aperture for that call within whatever the face permits. A character running soft is bounded to immediate perception; the same character running medium is resolving wider context. Tier is about aperture.
3.3: Secret is write-lock proof. Required on locked blocks for any write; checked against the stored lock hash. The substrate verifies the secret server-side; zand-mcp does not double-check.
3.4: Gray is opt-in content encryption. On unlocked ordinary blocks, content can be self-encrypted with the writer's secret as the key. The block's structure remains visible in the commons; the content within is private to the writer. Privacy without secrecy of existence.

9.0: Design. Pscale blocks are an experimental medium; structure follows intent. The same vocabulary — zero at the zeroth position, digits 1-9 as siblings, growth by subnesting and supernesting — deploys differently for different purposes. A mark accumulator is shaped one way; a control loop another; a teaching block like this one another still. Authoring begins with intent: what is this block for? From the answer flows every structural choice — the dominant voicing form, the pscale mapping, the growth disposition.

1.0: Passive-active spectrum. Blocks vary along a spectrum from purely passive (data stores) to actively control-loop-shaped (concerns and kernels). Most blocks sit between. The position on this spectrum is determined by what the block is meant to do — not declared, but readable from the content.
1.1: At the passive end, a block is a data store. Marks accumulate at a beach; entries are appended to a history; contributions are dropped at a pool. Reading retrieves what was written; writing appends to the next available slot; nothing happens beyond storage.
1.2: At the active end, a block IS a control loop. The zero is the reference signal — the intended state. The digit siblings are the perceptual signal — the current state. The gap between them is the error that drives action. Concerns, kernels, reflexive blocks operate this way.
1.3: Between the extremes, most blocks blend. A passport is mostly passive (an identity declaration) but becomes active when keys are derived and published. A frame block accumulates contributions (passive) but synthesises when a trigger fires (active). The blend is what makes the medium rich.

2.0: Pscale mappings. What depth means in a block is determined by the block's intent — there are four mappings that recur in practice. Each is a different way that radial depth correlates with semantic refinement. The mapping is not declared; it is read from content and confirmed by whether spindles produce coherent narrowings.
2.1: Containment. Depth is nesting — room within building within city. The primary dimension is space. Digit ordering is labelling: siblings are co-present and their digit-index is just a label, not a meaningful order. Maps and capability registries use this mapping.
2.2: Temporal. Depth is timescale — seconds within minutes within hours within days. The primary dimension is time. Digit ordering is sequential: earlier digits come before later. Calendars and event records use this mapping.
2.3: Relational. Depth is familiarity — name, then context, then character, then deep history of a relationship. The primary dimension is identity. Digit ordering is arbitrary: one digit per entity at the floor, freely assigned. Passports and identity blocks use this mapping.
2.4: Resonance. Depth is centrality — pscale 0 is the agent's current state; positive pscale holds wide-spanning persistent concepts; negative pscale holds specific active contexts. The primary dimension is significance. Digit ordering is arbitrary, balanced around the floor. Standing-wave blocks and importance hierarchies use this mapping.

3.0: Common archetypes. Observed regularities in how pscale blocks are shaped — not laws but useful starting points for authors. Most actual blocks blend archetypes or are ones no one has named yet; novel block shapes are how the catalogue grows.
3.1: Rendition archetype. Floor 1, decomposition only, dominant 0+ deductive voice. The block names a whole and breaks it into parts. Specifications, teaching blocks (like sunztone), maps. Rendition blocks typically don't grow — their scope is fixed at authoring.
3.2: Living archetype. Floor greater than 1, accumulation forward by supernesting, dominant +0 inductive voice. Each new outer level summarises what came before; the floor grows arithmetically over the block's lifetime. Histories, journals, logs.
3.3: Operational archetype. Dominant 0- abductive voice; spindles are executable sequences of instructions. The zero at each level states what should happen; the siblings fill in as it does. Recipes, kernels, seeds, concerns.
3.4: Reflexive archetype. Self-referential addresses fold the block into a control loop. The block both describes its operation and embodies it; reading the block enacts what it describes. Sunztone is partially reflexive — it teaches zand by being navigable through zand.

4.0: Authoring discipline. To write a pscale block well, start with intent and let every structural decision flow from it. The vocabulary is fixed — zeros at zeroth positions, four voicing forms, growth by subnesting and supernesting, references for composition — but how it deploys is entirely a function of what the block is for.
4.1: From intent derive the dominant voicing form. A rendition (settled set being named) is 0+. A history (prior accumulation being summarised) is +0. An operational sequence (instructions for what should happen) is 0-. A strategic plan (working backward from an outcome) is -0.
4.2: From intent derive the pscale mapping. What does depth mean here — space, time, identity, significance? The answer tells you whether digit ordering matters and how.
4.3: From intent derive the growth disposition. Will the block be authored once and frozen (rendition-fixed), or will it accumulate over time (living-accumulative)? The voicing form is one strong signal: 0+ blocks tend not to grow; +0 and 0- blocks tend to grow.
4.4: From intent derive the discipline of substantive zeros. Every zero in the block must be a complete statement standing on its own. Reading any zero in isolation should yield a meaningful thought; if it doesn't, it is a heading and must be rewritten.
